Abstract

This notebook contains exercises for participants of the EFP 2026 workshop on animal social network analsis.

# install the tidyverse package if not already on your laptop
if (!requireNamespace("tidyverse", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("tidyverse")
}

# load the package
library(tidyverse)

Exercise 1

The code chunk below produces 10 binary outcomes with a fixed probability \(p\). These outcomes can represent behaviours; e.g., whether individuals are foraging (\(Y = 1\)) or not (\(Y = 0\)) during one scan sample.

This generative model can be represented qualitatively using a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)—see Figure 1.

Run Generative model A a few times and observe what you obtain.

Exercise 1.1. (i) Change the probability of foraging such that this behaviour becomes rarer; (ii) increase the number of individuals to 120; (iii) increase the number of scans per individual to 12.

Exercise 1.2. Draw a DAG that is consistent with the generative model produced in Exercise 1.1.

Exercise 2

Consider the generative model below—a modified version of the Erdős–Rényi model.

This model can be represented as a DAG (Figure 2):

Exercise 2.1. Run Generative model B a couple of times and try to understand what each line does. What has changed compared to Generative model A?

Exercise 2.2. Instead of a fixed probability across dyads \((i, j)\), modify Generative model B so that different dyads have different probabilities of interacting. Specifically, draw the probabilities from a Beta distribution with parameters \(\alpha = 2\) and \(\beta = 2\). The rbeta() R function can help you do that.

Exercise 2.3. Similarly, change the code such that different dyads are observed for different amounts of time: draw sampling efforts \(S_{[i,j]}\) from a Poisson distribution with rate \(\lambda = 5\) periods per dyad on average, using the rpois() R function.

Exercise 2.4. Does the DAG change following the modifications brought by Exercises 2.2. and 2.3? If so, how? If not, why not?

Exercise 3

In this exercise, we will study the stochastic block model introduced during the workshop. Figure 3 shows the DAG corresponding to the corresponding data-generating process.

We start by defining the number of total scans that we perform, as well as the block matrix \(\theta\).

We then construct a data frame for individual- and dyad-level features.

Next, we have scan sampling. We write a for loop across scans: there is a fixed probability to observe an individual during any one scan.

Finally, we compute \(S_{[i,j]}\)—encoding the number of times that each dyad could have been observed interacting, or the number of “binomial trials”—, retrieve \(p_{[i,j]}\) for each dyad from the matrix \(\theta\), and draw the number of grooming events \(Y_{[i,j]}\) for each dyad.

Try to run and understand the code chunks above; i.e., Generative model C (1/4)-Generative model C (4/4).

Exercise 3.1. What information is encoded in df_dyad_scan[[7]]?

Exercise 3.2. Why, in Generative model C (4/4), did we write S_ij = sum(observed_i == 1 | observed_j == 1)? In other words, why did we need to see either \(i\) or \(j\) in a scan—instead of, for example, both \(i\) and \(j\)—to be counted as part of the sampling effort \(S_{[i,j]}\)?

Exercise 3.3. Suppose that individual sex were to affect sampling effort. How would you modify Figure 3 accordingly?

Exercise 3.4. How could you translate the causal effect of individual sex on \(S_{[i,j]}\) into code? Writing your response in English—for example, with reference to the relevant code chunks—is sufficient.
